result:
Surface meets sky where the jetty ends and the wind decides it has traveled far enough. I’ve been standing here long enough—ten, fifteen, thirty minutes—for the gulls to move from suspicion to irritation to a grudging, wing-tucked truce. They perch along the rusted rail as if it is a parliament of slate-feathered elders, and I am the lone human apostate who still believes answers float somewhere in the harbor haze. The tide is midway between giving and taking, so the pilings alternate between gleaming wet and barnacled exhaustion. Each one, I decide, is a sentence half spoken by the ocean to the sky: I was here, before you were. I shifted moods and containers and shipping lanes, but I was always here. Behind me the city’s stone-and-glass metaphor hums like a refrigerator; ahead of me is that thin, thunder-blue layer where light finally breaks free from all the things it has been bouncing off of.

Somewhere below, sand grains work their invisible tectonics, rolling millimeter constellations into dunes the way people rearrange shadows inside themselves. I imagine every grain has a serial number and a tiny history: dislodged from a Kirkland granite countertop, a 1997 beach volleyball court, a child’s sneaker sole, a tombstone worn illegible by acidic rain. All of them converging here, tasting salt because salt is an inherited memory. When I was nine I believed the ocean tasted like Coca-Cola left too long in sunlight, and nobody corrected me for years; the lie was too appealing, tingly and dark and slightly medicinal. The truth needed to wait until I mistakenly swallowed a mouthful in a wipeout: the slap and burn, honest, like chewing on pennies.

I once dated a woman who said the ocean is a punctuation mark—an em dash—between the continents. The sentence keeps trying to end but the dash insists on pause, on maybe. She was the sort of person who carried a fountain pen because she liked how the nib whispered hypotheses onto receipts. We shared the obsessions: obsolete atlases that still called Istanbul “Constantinople,” the precise moment hadrosaur bones turn from wood to stone, Arctic moss that remembers Vikings. We broke up when she moved north to study “glacial moraines as hoarded grievances.” I never could categorize my own grievances clearly enough; they slip between tides, refuse pile-ups, midnight key changes in unfinished piano scores.

The gulls are arguing again, two of them inventing a language entirely of swivels. Their outrage rises the way a whetted blade lifts when testing: is this sharp enough? Is this edge indifferent or merely precise? I think how human arguments are duller, mostly performed on balance sheets or comment threads where nobody’s feet get wet. We forget surprise, and surprise is the salt of living. Last week a woman on the subway, wrapped in neon headphones, suddenly looked up and sang to the car in Korean; people clapped, stunned by the audacity of song. Her voice had the shape of moonlit laundry snapping on a line—something intimate but everywhere. Then she exited at Union Square, vanishing like an erased lyric. That was salt too, the sting behind our eyes that turned, very briefly, to nutrition.

Gulls launch, one by one, toward the red channel marker. I follow their ellipse across the water which today resembles polished hematite. This morning I saw my own reflection there: boy, man, ghost-in-progress. The face was predictable—middle-parting hair going stubborn at the crown, a scar over the left brow from a childhood collision with a swing—but inside the eyes circled some feral green mess I didn’t want to name. I thought: am I looking up at myself or down? The water never resolves direction, only depth. I learned from a pilot friend that depth perception fails over monochrome landscapes—snow, sand, ocean—because the brain craves contrast. We misjudge distance and sometimes believe we can land where sky meets surface, a mirage joke lodged in our evolutionary firmware. We were built for myth and embarrassment in roughly equal measure.

The ferry sounds its baritone horn: one long vowel of departure, three shorter ones of serious reconsideration. The ropes are being thrown in circles that remind me of wedding rings returned. I’ve always envied rituals that come with choreography. There is dignity in the choreography of letting go: a rope uncoils, a bell rings, the dock recedes with the slow certainty of a clock that only tells distance. I wave without meaning to, not at passengers but at the version of me I keep cargoed on outbound vessels, a stowaway neatly folded between archived regrets.

I should mention the smell: diesel braided with kelp and warm sesame from a food truck I cannot see. Smell is the last fair witness. It refuses metaphor the way teeth refuse plastic. That sesame note threads me back to my grandmother’s kitchen on New Year’s Eve when she used a knitting needle as a chopstick to test if the oil was hot enough for youtiao. She never measured, only listened for the telltale hiss. Grandma said flour blooms when it knows you’re paying attention. I was eight and certain she held communion with invisible ingredients. After her funeral I sprinkled a teaspoon of flour onto her grave; it snowed later that night, the kind of coincidence poetry majors genuflect to.

I take a step closer to the edge where a final plank extends into nothingness. This jetty has warned generations of children in postage-stamp voices: Don’t slip, the barnacles will open your palm like soft fruit. Yet every summer someone leaves here with a constellation of new pink scars, proof they touched something ancient and got caught. I hold out my own palms. They look disappointingly intact, paper cut healing to a pale comma. The ocean performs its habitual hush-roar, muezzin to agnostic waves. It preaches spiral without resolution, alphabet without end. I think of all the words I never sent: letters aborted at paragraph two because I didn’t wish to bruise the white space with explanations. Silence can be a kindness or a hemorrhage; often both inside the same hour.

Out past the breakwater, a small red sailboat reclines at thirty degrees, practicing its surrender. The single sailor raises a can of something—beer, ginger, perhaps just sun—to me. I toast back with an imaginary glass full of sea-spray. Any minute now the fog will tire of flirting with the skyline and swallow it, digesting cranes into cartoon outlines. The seagulls have settled again, having apparently ruled on whatever urgent motion was deliberated. I picture their vote tally etched into guano streaks: Yea: 7, Nay: 4, Abstentions: the Tao.

I think about how journeys are cheating when they only go halfway. We leave home, march across thresholds, invest in tickets and new shoes, but somewhere mid-ocean we upholster nostalgia, refurbish clichés, and turn back. Still, the small red boat keeps going. I imagine its hull stitched from<|reserved_token_163792|> Grandma’s flour sacks, rising—absurd—until it’s less vessel than kite. At that point I won’t know if I’m imagining it, or if the sky itself has agreed to host impossible traffic.

My phone buzzes: another update I do not read. Instead I note the time by the angle of the lighthouse shadow across the jetty nails. I breathe hundred-year air recycled by lobsters, kestrels, fermenting sailcloth. I whisper a line I once read on a bathroom wall in Reykjavik: “The ocean doesn’t apologize; it deepens.” Then I say a different, original line aloud to good company: barnacles, paper cups, uneaten fries, microscopic plankton spinning galaxies on their flagella. Somewhere recorded, maybe, on gull retina or buoy sonar. Maybe not. Either way I have paid my toll: 1000 words, give or take the unsayable stretch between two landings.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
